body_txt: So a villain decides they want to hurt the hero and default to the tried and true method of kidnapping a loved one . They select one innocent bystander or who appears to be the weakest and inconsequential member of the team, figuring it's perfect leverage. The problem is, everyone suddenly shows up to rescue them. The villain chose the one person who would piss off everyone from The Paragon to the Ineffectual Loner who normally stays out of the fight. Congratulations, you’ve just found Everyone's Baby Sister (or Brother). When the Big Bad threatens this character, it becomes personal for every one else. It didn't occur that this may well make the heroes more dangerous, not less . They created a Conflict Killer by targeting the wrong person. Everyone's Baby Sister is most common in works where there is a regular sense of danger along with Headbutting Heroes who aren't always on the same page but the character exacerbates their protective instincts, especially if the individual is working in safer environments and not expected to be at risk of physical harm. A team composed of law enforcement officials and scientists or analysts is common here. This character will typically be a younger character who is not usually in any sort of physical danger and, thus, may appear physically smaller and weaker than the other characters. Other will feel protective of this character because of a combination of their older age, superior physical strength or occupational responsibility. Familiar relation is common, paired with a Big Brother Instinct . When something happens to a character and even the Jerkass is unsettled by it, you have a winner. Sometimes these characters inspire this reaction because they’re impossible to dislike and spend much of the time as the peacekeeper of the group . Other times, the character really isn’t very likable at all but their immaturity is explained by their youth . Because main characters tend to be the Action Heroes , this character is more often a supporting character than a lead (although it's not impossible for an equally heroic character to fit this trope by being The One with a Personal Life ). By coincidence or design, these characters are often dorky Teen Genius types (or early-to-mid-twenties genius, as the case may be), though this is certainly not a prerequisite. These characters also tend to be fan favorites , though this, again, is by no means, necessary. It is not unheard of for The Scrappy to fall under this trope. What’s important here is the reaction of the characters, not of the audience. Compare and contrast The Heart , The Cutie , the Tagalong Kid , Moe , And Your Little Dog, Too! . Note that this character does not actually have to be anyone’s sister, and a character who is someone’s sister is not guaranteed to fall under this trope.
